title: Quadrella ferruginea
intro: Quadrella ferruginea, also known as Mexican prickly poppy, is a plant species in the Brassicaceae family, commonly found in tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, including its ability to treat various health issues such as fever, rheumatism, and respiratory problems, due to its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Quadrella ferruginea include alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. In traditional medicine, the plant is often prepared as a decoction, infusion, or tincture, and is also used in the form of capsules or tablets to leverage its health benefits.
taxonomy: Quadrella ferruginea is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Capparaceae, which is a group of plants commonly known as the caper family. This classification is based on its morphological characteristics and genetic relationships with other plants in the same family. Quadrella ferruginea is a member of the genus Quadrella, which is a group of plants native to the Americas. The plant is also known to be closely related to other species in the Capparaceae family, such as the caper plant. The classification of Quadrella ferruginea is an ongoing area of research in botany.

Its roots and leaves are employed in treating various conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and skin infections. The plant is also believed to have antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties.
